lucius_sulla_convo_1_n01.npcDialogue: The tribal assembly voted this morning. Sulpicius bought it with Italian freedmen — herded them through the gates like cattle to a market. My command — assigned by the Senate, ratified by precedent, mine by every definition the Republic still pretends to honor — has been handed to a seventy-year-old man who can barely climb the Capitoline steps. So. I have a question, Crispus, and I'm asking you before I ask Rome: when the law is used to destroy the law, does a lawful man submit — or does he act?

options.playerDialogue: He acts. If the Republic can't defend its own instruments, the men who serve it must.
Marching legions on Rome doesn't restore the law, Sulla. It buries it. There has to be something else.
That depends entirely on what you're about to ask me to do.

lucius_sulla_convo_1_n02.npcDialogue: There it is. I've had this conversation six times today — six officers, six men who said the right words and then went pale at what the words meant. You didn't go pale. I noticed. The legions at Nola are already waiting for my order. They've been waiting, if I'm honest. Soldiers understand this faster than senators do.

lucius_sulla_convo_1_n03.npcDialogue: Something else. I love that phrase — it's where good men go to hide from necessary ones. Tell me, Crispus — what does 'something else' look like when the tribunes have already voted, the Senate has been overruled, and every legal mechanism has been fed through Marius's purse? I am asking sincerely. I looked for another door. There isn't one.

responses.playerDialogue: Force a senatorial re-vote with the patrician quorum present. Make every man in that chamber choose — Marius or constitutional order — with his name attached.
Split the command. You take the east, give Marius the Italian campaign. Let him have something so he doesn't swallow everything.
You're right — there isn't one. I was testing whether you'd hesitate when you said it. You didn't.
Kill Sulpicius. Without the tribune, the legislation dies with him. Quieter than a siege, and the precedent is deniable.

fallbackResponse.playerDialogue: I don't have one. But legions in the streets — that can't be the answer either.

lucius_sulla_convo_1_n04.npcDialogue: I'm asking you to choose a side of a wall. When my legions reach the Esquiline Gate — and they will reach it — you will be inside with Marius or outside with me. That is the entire universe of options. Neutrality died this morning when Sulpicius cast his vote.

options.playerDialogue: Then tell me the plan. All of it — not the version for senators.

lucius_sulla_convo_1_n05.npcDialogue: Six legions from Nola. Two columns — Esquiline and Colline gates, simultaneously. My standing orders are explicit: no looting, no civilian slaughter. This is a restoration, not a sack. I want that distinction to survive the histories. Marius and Sulpicius will be declared hostes — enemies of the state. But I need someone inside the city first. Someone who knows which senators will fold when they see eagles in the Forum and which ones will need... persuading. Can you build me that list?

options.playerDialogue: I already know half of it. Give me two days and I'll know the rest — every name, every breaking point.
Give me a sword instead. I'd rather be at the gate than behind a writing desk.

lucius_sulla_convo_1_n06.npcDialogue: Clever suggestions, all of them. Genuinely. But you're solving yesterday's problem — events moved past that door sometime around dawn.

lucius_sulla_convo_1_n07.npcDialogue: The march happens with or without your blessing — I hope that's clear. What I'm offering you is a position, not a vote. When I enter Rome, there will be proscription lists. Names written in very permanent ink. I can add names to that list and I can remove them. Where you stand in the next seventy-two hours determines which side of the ink you're on. I say this with — I promise you — complete affection.

options.playerDialogue: I'm with you. Put me where I'm most useful.
I'll step aside. I won't oppose you — but I won't carry your lists either.
Proscription lists. Listen to yourself, Sulla — the Republic you claim to be saving doesn't do this.

lucius_sulla_convo_1_n08.npcDialogue: When this is finished, I will restore the constitution — every law, every magistracy, every check and balance that Marius corrupted. That is my oath, and I keep my oaths. The men who stood with me will be remembered the way the Republic remembers its defenders — with land, with honors, with the quiet satisfaction of having been right. You chose well, Crispus. I'm glad. Genuinely glad.

lucius_sulla_convo_1_n09.npcDialogue: Then you've chosen. I gave you every opportunity — more than most, because I liked you. Remember that, when the gates open and you see your name on a list. I liked you, and I gave you the chance, and you decided to be principled instead of alive. We won't speak as equals again.

lucius_sulla_convo_1_n10.npcDialogue: Ah — standing aside. The geometry of cowardice. You know, I almost admire it as a technique. Almost. When the new order arrives, don't expect the privileges you enjoyed under the old one. Neutrality isn't a position, Crispus. It's just a slower way of losing.

operations.reason: Committed to Sulla's march on Rome
You are now a marked enemy of the Marian faction
The Italians see Sulla's faction as their enemy

deltas.reason: Joined the first march on Rome in Republican history

operations.reason: Sulla marks you as a public enemy
Your defiance of Sulla earns respect from the Marians

deltas.reason: Refused to participate in the destruction of Republican norms
